evidence_quotes.quote: consumers retain permanent access to digital games for which they have paid, regardless of whether online servers are subsequently switched off.
Obligation to provide an offline mode for games with single players’ content before discontinuing online operations.
Obligation to publish server software or technical means enabling communities to operate their own servers, e.g. in online games without offline alternatives.
Transparent labelling obligation when buying: Buyers must clearly identify whether a game without a server becomes useless.
Legal enshrining that switching off a product for which the user has paid once is only permitted under clearly defined conditions.

paragraphs.text: I call for the Digital Fairness Act to make sure that consumers retain permanent access to digital games for which they have paid, regardless of whether online servers are subsequently switched off. At present, it is legally possible that even with single players components, games may be rendered completely unplayable by server shutdowns, despite the fact that consumers have bought these products. This is not only unfair but also a systematic breach of fundamental principles of consumer protection. Digital purchases must not bear an expiry date. I therefore call for: Obligation to provide an offline mode for games with single players’ content before discontinuing online operations. Obligation to publish server software or technical means enabling communities to operate their own servers, e.g. in online games without offline alternatives. Transparent labelling obligation when buying: Buyers must clearly identify whether a game without a server becomes useless. Legal enshrining that switching off a product for which the user has paid once is only permitted under clearly defined conditions. These demands correspond to the core concerns of the international consumer initiative Stop Killing Games, which was able to collect over 1.4 million signatures in the EU in July 2025. The initiative was created after Ubisoft completely deactivated The Crew (2014) despite the existence of single players, a decision affecting thousands of players. The practice of making games subsequently unusable endangers not only the rights of consumers but also the cultural preservation of digital media. Video games are an important part of our digital cultural heritage and deserve to remain usable in the long term. I therefore expressly support the integration of these principles into the Digital Fairness Act. The aim must be to treat digital products in the same way as physical ones: Anyone who buys a game should also be allowed to play it today, tomorrow and in ten years’ time. Please take these concerns seriously. The EU has the chance to be at the forefront of digital consumer protection. Thanks.
